title: Foraging for natural dye plants
subtitle: Identifying and gathering common dye plants from hedgerows, gardens, and verges
excerpt: Some of the most reliable natural dye materials are growing in British hedgerows, roadsides, and gardens right now. This guide covers eight plants that are commonly available, identifiable without specialist knowledge, and productive enough to use for a first or second dye project. For each: when to gather, which part to use, what colour to expect, and any relevant caution.
sourceNotes:
- Jenny Dean, Wild Color: UK dye plant identification, seasons, colour results
- Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes: weld, woad, bracken, elderberry dye chemistry
- Richard Mabey, Food for Free: UK foraging law summary and plant identification
- Plantlife, Flora of Great Britain and Ireland: woad distribution and legal status notes

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt (such as alum or iron) used to chemically bond dye to fibre, improving both colour uptake and washfastness.
A naturally occurring astringent compound in many plants; acts as a mild mordant on its own and assists alum mordants on cellulose fibres.
Reseda luteola, one of Europe's oldest yellow dye plants; gives a clear, lightfast yellow on alum-mordanted wool.
Isatis tinctoria, a historic blue dye plant once cultivated widely in Britain; rare in the wild now but still found on disturbed chalk ground.
A dyed fibre's resistance to fading from light, washing, or rubbing; expressed on a scale of 1-5 in textile testing, with 5 being most resistant.

content.text: Natural dyeing does not require specialist suppliers for every project. Many of the plants used historically for textile colouring are still common in the British countryside and in ordinary gardens. Knowing what to look for, when to gather it, and which part of the plant holds the colour will let you build a seasonal dye pantry from your surroundings at almost no cost. A good alum
mordant
on the fibre is still needed for most of these plants; foraging supplies the dye material, not the mordant chemistry.
Wear gloves when handling unknown plants. Never eat foraged material intended for dyeing. Keep dye vessels separate from food use.
UK foraging law: what you need to know

Plants on private land (including farmland) require the landowner's permission before gathering. On public rights of way, verges, and hedgerows, common law allows small personal quantities of leaves, flowers, and berries. Never uproot a wild plant: this is an offence under the Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981 regardless of where you are. Gather no more than you will use within the week.
Weld
Reseda luteola is one of the most reliable yellow dye plants in Europe.
Weld

grows on disturbed chalk and limestone ground, road verges, and waste ground across southern England. Look for it June through August as a tall (60-120 cm) spike of small pale-yellow flowers. Gather the whole above-ground plant (stem, leaves, flowers) when in full flower. Colour: clear, lightfast yellow on alum-mordanted wool; one of the best
colour fastness
results of any native yellow plant. Safety: no significant hazards.
Woad

Isatis tinctoria, the historic British blue dye plant, is now rare in the wild: it occurs on a handful of chalk and limestone sites, mainly in Gloucestershire and Surrey. It is legal to gather leaves in small quantities from sites where it grows freely (not nature reserves), but do not uproot.
Woad

leaves give a weak blue (much less indigo content than cultivated indigo) and require a reduction vat as for indigo. Gather fresh leaves in spring or early summer from the first-year rosette. More reliably obtained as seed to grow in a garden pot than foraged in the wild.
Bracken

Pteridium aquilinum is abundant at woodland edges and on heath across the UK. Gather young fronds (fiddleheads) in spring, or older fronds through summer. The whole frond is used. Colour: soft, warm yellow-green to buff on alum-mordanted wool; more muted than weld but easy to source in large quantities. Safety: bracken contains ptaquiloside (a carcinogen) and thiaminase; wear gloves when handling and do not use cooking vessels for the dye bath. The dye bath itself is safe with gloves.
Elderberry
Sambucus nigra berries give a purple-pink on wool, though the colour is not particularly lightfast without a
tannin

pre-bath or a chrome mordant (which is not recommended for home use). Gather ripe berries in August and September from hedgerow elder. Use the berries only, not leaves or bark, which contain sambunigrin and are mildly toxic. For a modest improvement in lightfastness, add a small amount of tannin (oak galls, strong black tea) to the dye bath. Best used for samples and exploration rather than projects requiring a long-lasting colour.
Goldenrod

Canadian goldenrod (Solidago canadensis) grows widely on disturbed ground, railway banks, and waste land across Britain. Gather it in flower from late July through September. Use the whole above-ground plant. Colour: strong yellow to yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool, with lightfastness as good as weld. Safety: no significant hazards, though some people have pollen allergies. Wear a light mask if cutting large amounts in full flower.
Nettles

Urtica dioica is everywhere from March through October. Gather young tops (the top four to six leaves) in spring for the highest chlorophyll content. Colour: soft, slightly khaki yellow-green on alum-mordanted wool; not a vivid result but useful for greened neutrals. Nettles lose much of their colour on drying; use fresh for the best result. Safety: wear thick rubber gloves to harvest; the sting is neutralised by simmering.
Birch leaves

Betula pendula leaves give a clear, warm yellow on alum-mordanted wool and are available in large quantities from May through early autumn. Gather spring leaves for the most vivid yellow; later-season leaves produce a more olive result. Use fresh leaves. Safety: no significant hazards.
Blackberry (leaves and bark)
Rubus fruticosus leaves and young canes give a grey to buff colour on
tannin

-rich yarn with no mordant required, or a beige-yellow on alum-mordanted wool. The berries give pink-purple (similar limitations to elderberry). Leaves and canes are available throughout the growing season. Safety: handle with thick gloves because of the thorns.
Storing foraged material

Use fresh material within a week, or dry it for long-term storage. To dry: spread in a single layer on a clean surface away from direct sunlight and in good airflow; turn daily until completely dry (typically 3-5 days in a warm room or 7-10 days at ambient temperature). Dried plant material keeps in paper bags or loose-woven cloth bags (not sealed plastic) for up to a year. Label with plant name and harvest date.
